title: RxJS Foundations
narrativeIntro: The Drift speaks in streams. Not single values but continuous flows of data — events, HTTP responses, user interactions, time itself. RxJS is the language of these streams: Observables that emit, operators that transform, subscriptions that listen. The most powerful pattern in Angular lives here.
narrativeOutro: Streams. Flows. Reactive patterns. You're no longer waiting for data — you're responding to it as it arrives, transforming it in flight, composing it into new streams. The Drift's final region pulses with current.
phmConnectionNote: Ryland learns to process continuous streams of data from the astrophage. You learn RxJS basics.
conceptsPreview: of(), pipe(), map/filter operators, Subject, subscription cleanup, takeUntil

challenges.title: Stream Output
challenges.narrativeHook: A stream of numbers passes through two operators. Predict what arrives at the subscriber.
challenges.instructions:
An Observable emits the values `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]`. A `filter` keeps only even numbers. A `map` doubles each remaining value.

Predict what the subscriber receives.
challenges.solution:
// No code changes — this is a prediction challenge.
// Answer: 4, 8  (filter keeps 2 and 4, map doubles them to 4 and 8)
challenges.predictPrompt: What values does the subscriber receive?

explainGate.prompt: What is the difference between filter and map in RxJS, and in what order do operators in pipe() execute?

hints: filter(n => n % 2 === 0) keeps only 2 and 4 from [1,2,3,4,5].
pipe() executes operators left-to-right. filter runs first, then map.

challenges.title: Transform the Stream
challenges.narrativeHook: Raw sensor readings arrive. Filter the noise. Format what remains.
challenges.instructions:
Complete `StreamProcessor.process()` using a real RxJS pipeline.

- Call `of(...values)` to create an Observable from the array
- Chain `.pipe(filter(...), map(...))` to transform the stream
- `filter`: keep only values above 50
- `map`: format each as `'READING: ' + value`
- `.subscribe(result => this.output.push(result))` to collect results

This is the real RxJS pattern: `import { of } from 'rxjs'` and `import { filter, map } from 'rxjs/operators'`.

files.name: stream-processor.ts

tests.testCode: const p = new StreamProcessor(); p.process([45, 62, 38, 71, 55]); if (p.output.length !== 3) throw new Error(`Expected 3 outputs, got ${p.output.length}`);
const p = new StreamProcessor(); p.process([45, 62, 38, 71, 55]); if (!p.output.includes('READING: 62')) throw new Error(`output should include 'READING: 62', got ${JSON.stringify(p.output)}`);
const p = new StreamProcessor(); p.process([45, 62, 38, 71, 55]); if (p.output.some(s => s.includes('45') || s.includes('38'))) throw new Error('Values 45 and 38 should be filtered out');
const p = new StreamProcessor(); p.process([10, 20]); if (p.output.length !== 0) throw new Error(`All values below 50 should be filtered, got ${p.output.length} outputs`);

hints: Start with of(...values) — the spread operator turns the array into individual emissions.
Chain .pipe(filter(v => v > 50), map(v => 'READING: ' + v)) — operators run left-to-right inside pipe().
End with .subscribe(result => this.output.push(result)) — nothing runs until subscribe() is called.

challenges.title: Subscription Leak
challenges.narrativeHook: The component is destroyed. The interval keeps running. Memory fills with ghosts.
challenges.instructions:
This component subscribes to `interval(100)` — a real RxJS creation operator that emits an incrementing counter every 100ms. When the component is destroyed, the subscription keeps running, leaking memory.

There are exactly two fixes:
1. Store the subscription: `this.sub = readings$.subscribe(...)`
2. Unsubscribe in `ngOnDestroy`: `this.sub.unsubscribe()`

In a real app: `import { interval, Subscription } from 'rxjs'`

files.name: stream.component.ts

tests.description: StreamComponent should store the subscription in this.sub
tests.testCode: const c = new StreamComponent(); c.ngOnInit(); if (!c.sub) throw new Error('subscription should be stored in this.sub — assign the return value of readings$.subscribe()');
const c = new StreamComponent(); c.ngOnInit(); c.ngOnDestroy(); if (!c.sub.closed) throw new Error('subscription should be closed after ngOnDestroy — call this.sub.unsubscribe()');

explainGate.prompt: What is a subscription memory leak in Angular, and why is ngOnDestroy the right place to unsubscribe from an interval() Observable?

hints: Store the return value of .subscribe(): this.sub = readings$.subscribe(...);
In ngOnDestroy, call this.sub.unsubscribe() — interval() keeps running until something unsubscribes.
Declare the property: this.sub = null; in the constructor, then assign it in ngOnInit.

challenges.title: Event Bus
challenges.narrativeHook: Components need to communicate without knowing each other. A shared Subject is the bridge.
challenges.instructions:
Complete the `EventBusService` using a real RxJS `Subject`.

- `emit(event)`: push `event` to `this._history`, then call `this._bus.next(event)`
- `on(handler)`: subscribe to `this._bus` and return the subscription
- `get history()`: return a copy of `this._history`

In a real app: `import { Subject } from 'rxjs'`

The Subject API: `new Subject()`, `.next(value)`, `.subscribe(handler)` — identical to what you see here.

files.name: event-bus.service.ts

tests.description: emit() should add events to history
tests.testCode: const s = new EventBusService(); s.emit('launch'); s.emit('abort'); if (s.history.length !== 2) throw new Error(`history should have 2 items, got ${s.history.length}`);

tests.description: on() handler should receive emitted events
tests.testCode: const s = new EventBusService(); const received = []; s.on(e => received.push(e)); s.emit('ping'); s.emit('pong'); if (received.length !== 2 || received[0] !== 'ping') throw new Error(`handler should receive events, got ${JSON.stringify(received)}`);

tests.description: on() should return a subscription with .unsubscribe()
tests.testCode: const s = new EventBusService(); const sub = s.on(() => {}); if (!sub || typeof sub.unsubscribe !== 'function') throw new Error('on() should return a subscription with .unsubscribe()');

hints: emit(): push to history first, then notify: this._history.push(event); this._bus.next(event);
on(): delegate directly to the Subject: return this._bus.subscribe(handler);
history getter: return a copy so callers can't mutate internal state: return [...this._history];

challenges.title: Automatic Cleanup
challenges.narrativeHook: Manual unsubscribe works. But Angular has a cleaner way. One Subject can close everything at once.
challenges.instructions:
Complete `StreamComponent` using the `takeUntil(this.destroy$)` pattern — the standard Angular approach for automatic subscription cleanup.

`ngOnInit()`: subscribe to `this.service.getStream()` using `.pipe(takeUntil(this.destroy$))`, push each value to `this.values`

`ngOnDestroy()`: call `this.destroy$.next()` then `this.destroy$.complete()` — this signals all `takeUntil` operators to complete automatically

In a real app:
```
import { Subject, takeUntil } from 'rxjs';
```

This pattern lets you manage many subscriptions with a single `destroy$` Subject instead of tracking each one.

files.name: stream.component.ts

tests.testCode: const source = new Subject(); const c = new StreamComponent(new DataStreamService(source)); c.ngOnInit(); source.next(10); source.next(20); if (c.values.length !== 2) throw new Error(`Expected 2 values before destroy, got ${c.values.length}`);

tests.description: values are NOT received after ngOnDestroy
tests.testCode: const source = new Subject(); const c = new StreamComponent(new DataStreamService(source)); c.ngOnInit(); source.next(10); c.ngOnDestroy(); source.next(20); if (c.values.length !== 1) throw new Error(`Expected 1 value after destroy, got ${c.values.length} — takeUntil should stop emissions after ngOnDestroy`);

tests.description: all subsequent emissions are blocked after ngOnDestroy
tests.testCode: const source = new Subject(); const c = new StreamComponent(new DataStreamService(source)); c.ngOnInit(); source.next(1); c.ngOnDestroy(); source.next(2); source.next(3); if (c.values.length !== 1) throw new Error(`Expected exactly 1 value, got ${c.values.length}`);

explainGate.prompt: How does takeUntil(this.destroy$) work, and why is calling destroy$.next() in ngOnDestroy enough to clean up all subscriptions that use this pattern?

hints: In ngOnDestroy: call this.destroy$.next() to signal completion, then this.destroy$.complete() to close the Subject itself.
takeUntil watches destroy$ — when destroy$ emits (via .next()), takeUntil automatically completes the source Observable, cleaning up the subscription without any manual unsubscribe.
